title: First Council of Nicaea
issue: Arianism and the full divinity of the Son
summary: The first ecumenical council answered the claim that the Son was a creature and confessed that the Son is truly God from God.
outcome: Produced the Nicene Creed's confession of the Son as begotten, not made, and of one essence with the Father.
whyItMatters: Nicaea protects the gospel itself: if Christ is not truly God, then worship, salvation, baptism, and prayer lose their Christian center.

keyTerms.definition: The teaching associated with Arius that the Son was made and therefore not eternal God in the same sense as the Father.
Of one essence; the word used to confess that the Son shares the same divine being as the Father.
The Son is eternally from the Father, not a creature brought into existence.

studyPath.title: The crisis
studyPath.detail: The Church had to answer whether Jesus Christ is truly divine or the greatest of created beings.

studyPath.title: The confession
studyPath.detail: The council used precise language to guard biblical worship of the Son.

studyPath.title: The reception
studyPath.detail: The council did not end the controversy instantly; Athanasius and later fathers defended its meaning.

title: First Council of Constantinople
issue: The Holy Spirit, Nicene faith, and lingering Arian controversy
summary: Constantinople reaffirmed Nicaea and clarified the Church's confession of the Holy Spirit as Lord and giver of life.
outcome: Expanded the creed's language about the Holy Spirit and strengthened Nicene Trinitarian doctrine.
whyItMatters: This council completes the classic Trinitarian confession used in worship: Father, Son, and Holy Spirit are glorified together.

keyTerms.definition: Opponents of the divinity of the Holy Spirit.
The confession that the Son is truly God, now clarified together with the divinity of the Spirit.
A form of praise that reveals the Church's doctrine through worship.

studyPath.title: Reaffirm Nicaea
studyPath.detail: The council guarded Nicaea against reinterpretations that weakened Christ's divinity.

studyPath.title: Confess the Spirit
studyPath.detail: The Spirit is not an impersonal force but Lord, giver of life, and worthy of worship.

studyPath.title: Read the Cappadocians
studyPath.detail: Basil, Gregory Nazianzen, and Gregory Nyssa provide the theological background.

title: Council of Ephesus
issue: Nestorian controversy and the unity of Christ's person
summary: Ephesus defended the unity of Christ and confessed Mary as Theotokos because the one born of her is truly God the Son incarnate.
outcome: Rejected Nestorian division of Christ and affirmed Theotokos as a Christological confession.
whyItMatters: The title Theotokos is not mainly about Mary in isolation; it protects the truth that Jesus is one divine person, not two subjects loosely joined.

keyTerms.definition: God-bearer; Mary is mother of the incarnate Son, who is truly God.
A Christological error associated with dividing Christ's humanity and divinity too sharply.
The unity of Christ's person: the eternal Son is the subject of both divine and human actions.

studyPath.title: Begin with Christ
studyPath.detail: The debate is about who Jesus is, not only about Marian language.

studyPath.title: Understand Theotokos
studyPath.detail: The title guards the unity of the incarnate Son.

studyPath.title: Trace later tensions
studyPath.detail: Ephesus is crucial background for Chalcedon and later Eastern divisions.

title: Council of Chalcedon
issue: How to confess Christ as fully God and fully man
summary: Chalcedon gave classic language for confessing one and the same Christ in two natures, divine and human.
outcome: Defined Christ as one person in two natures, without confusion, change, division, or separation.
whyItMatters: Chalcedon protects both sides of salvation: only true God can save, and only true man can heal humanity from within.

keyTerms.definition: Christ is fully divine and fully human.
The divine and human natures belong to one and the same Son.
A label often used for views that collapse Christ's humanity and divinity into one nature; the historical use is complex.

studyPath.title: Read the definition
studyPath.detail: The four negative phrases prevent both mixing and splitting Christ.

studyPath.title: Connect to salvation
studyPath.detail: Christ's full divinity and full humanity are necessary for redemption.

studyPath.title: Study later reception
studyPath.detail: Chalcedon shaped Catholic, Eastern Orthodox, and Oriental Orthodox history in different ways.

title: Second Council of Constantinople
issue: Chalcedonian reception, anti-Nestorian clarification, and the Three Chapters
summary: The fifth council sought to show that Chalcedon did not authorize Nestorian division and that orthodox Christology remained faithful to Cyril.
outcome: Condemned the Three Chapters and clarified the interpretation of Chalcedon in an anti-Nestorian direction.
whyItMatters: This council matters because it shows how councils are received and interpreted, not merely announced.

keyTerms.definition: Writings and authors associated with positions judged too close to Nestorian division.
The Christological emphasis associated with Cyril of Alexandria on the unity of the incarnate Word.
The process by which the Church understands, defends, and applies a council's teaching.

studyPath.title: Return to Ephesus
studyPath.detail: The council cannot be understood apart from the earlier defense of Christ's unity.

studyPath.title: Read Chalcedon carefully
studyPath.detail: Constantinople II insists that two natures does not mean two Sons.

studyPath.title: Watch reception
studyPath.detail: The council caused controversy because it touched earlier compromises and regional trust.

title: Third Council of Constantinople
summary: The sixth council confessed that Christ has both a divine will and a human will, corresponding to his two natures.
outcome: Rejected monothelitism and affirmed that Christ's human will is real, obedient, and not swallowed up by his divinity.
whyItMatters: If Christ lacks a human will, then human obedience is not healed. The council protects the full humanity of the Savior.

keyTerms.definition: The teaching that Christ has only one will.
The confession that Christ has two wills, divine and human, united in one person.
Christ heals human willing by freely obeying the Father as man.

studyPath.title: Start at Gethsemane
studyPath.detail: Christ's prayer shows a real human will obedient to the Father.

studyPath.title: Connect to Chalcedon
studyPath.detail: Two natures imply the real operations proper to each nature.

studyPath.title: Study salvation
studyPath.detail: What Christ assumes, he heals; that includes human willing.

title: Second Council of Nicaea
calledBy: Empress Irene and Emperor Constantine VI
issue: Iconoclasm and the veneration of holy images
summary: The seventh council defended the veneration of icons by grounding Christian images in the reality of the incarnation.
outcome: Distinguished worship due to God alone from the honor shown to icons, saints, and holy images.
whyItMatters: Nicaea II argues that because the Word truly became visible flesh, Christian art can confess the incarnation rather than deny it.

keyTerms.definition: The rejection or destruction of religious images.
Honor shown to holy persons or images; distinct from worship owed to God alone.
The person represented by an image; honor passes to the one depicted, not to wood or paint as such.

studyPath.title: Begin with the incarnation
studyPath.detail: The defense of icons begins with the Word becoming visible.

studyPath.title: Distinguish worship and honor
studyPath.detail: The council separates adoration of God from veneration of holy images.

studyPath.title: Connect to devotion
studyPath.detail: Icons teach, remember, and direct attention toward Christ and his saints.
